text: The surgeon begins with a preperitoneal dissection and transitions to a retromuscular dissection, focusing on separating tissue layers in the abdominal region.
The surgeon observes a thin peritoneum, particularly around the patient's prior midline and groin incisions, indicating difficulty in maintaining a preperitoneal space during dissection.
The surgical team transitions to a retromuscular approach approximately three to five centimeters from the initial dissection point.
The surgeon performs a retromuscular dissection, extending down to the hernia. The dissection progresses three to four centimeters into the hernia space, separating the hernia sac from the pseudosac.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lower portion of the hernia, taking precautions to avoid the bladder, while the upper portion of the hernia is visually confirmed to be clear of any involvement.
The surgeon navigates through non-dense adhesions, identifying areolar dissection planes to smoothly reduce the entire hernia sac.

The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the bladder as it becomes involved in the surgical field. The bladder is carefully dissected away from the surrounding tissue down to the inferior margin of the hernia.

Following the completion of the midline dissection, the surgeon focuses on lateral dissection, exposing the bilateral myopectineal orifices. On the right side, the round ligament is transected close to the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery during preperitoneal dissection around the myopectineal orifice and transects it. On the left side, the round ligament is also identified.
The surgeon transects the tissue and works to develop a clear view of the myopectineal orifice bilaterally, revealing a prominent flap and a large defect in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses permanent V-lock sutures to perform placation of the hernia sac, followed by a definitive closure of the linea alba.
The surgeon demonstrates placating the hernia sac to eliminate dead space and reduce seroma formation, while preparing for easier closure of the widened fascial edges.

The surgeon demonstrates the plication of the linea alba cephalad, showing the fascial edges coming together with good approximation. The abdominal wall is fortified, and the final knee lock suture ensures the rectus muscles are closely approximated.
The surgeon completes the defect closure smoothly, with the tissue edges coming together better than expected.
The surgeon prepares for the next step of the procedure, which involves placing a surgical mesh over the previously closed fascial defect.

The surgeon places three pieces of mesh to cover the surgical area: bilateral groins are covered with Bard-Duty Maxx cells made of macroporous polypropylene, and the midline is covered with a mesh to span the entire space.

The surgeon fixates the mesh at multiple points during a transabdominal retromuscular repair, securing it to Cooper's ligament and various locations on the abdominal wall. Fiber and sealant are applied to enhance the mesh-tissue interface.
The surgeon performs flap closure, similar to the technique used for a single flap or bilateral inguinal hernia repair, ensuring the tissue layers are securely approximated.
The surgeon closes the flap, addressing fenestrations on the right side. The flap closure proceeds smoothly without tension, following the reduction of a large hernia.
